description: Test image registry for Image Describer Test Harness.

context.subject: Engineering
context.objective: Understand the First Law of Thermodynamics applied to closed systems

visualFacts.claim: The piston is a grey disc or circular element
A blue arrow appears above/near the piston as a separate element
Q label appears on the left side of the diagram
P.V label appears at the upper right, with an arrow pointing toward the piston region
U label appears at the lower right, with an arrow pointing into the gas/particle region
The blue arrow above the piston points upward, indicating the direction of work output
A system boundary is indicated
Red dots are contained within the lower portion of the cylinder, below the piston
The cylinder is drawn in 3D perspective, not as a flat 2D shape
The Q label is accompanied by a red arrow pointing rightward toward the cylinder
The cylinder has an open top with an elliptical opening through which the piston can move

context.subject: Engineering
context.objective: Understand the First Law of Thermodynamics applied to closed systems

visualFacts.claim: A thin/narrow strip or region between two close vertical lines, not a large shaded area
visualFacts.failPattern: entire area.*shaded|full area.*shaded|shaded region between.*state|hatching across

visualFacts.claim: The area under the curve is not shaded or coloured
visualFacts.failPattern: shaded area|shaded region|coloured region|shading under|shading beneath|is shaded|light grey shad|area is shaded|region is shaded

visualFacts.claim: Lines in the diagram are solid, not dashed
State 2 appears to the left of state 1 (unconventional ordering)
Arrows on the thin strip point inward, not outward
Pressure on vertical axis, Volume on horizontal axis
Tick marks on the vertical line segment are horizontal lines crossing through it, not dimension-line marks on either side
visualFacts.failPattern: dimension line|marks on either side|ticks on either side|serifs on either side

visualFacts.claim: The curve is concave upward (curving toward the origin), not concave downward
visualFacts.failPattern: concave down|curving away from the origin

visualFacts.claim: Small filled dots appear on the horizontal axis at states 1 and 2
The δV strip is positioned between states 2 and 1, closer to state 2 (left side)
visualFacts.failPattern: closer to state 1|near state 1|adjacent to state 1

visualFacts.claim: The thin strip is labelled δV (or dV)
The vertical axis is labelled P
The horizontal axis is labelled V

ga-mechanism.name: Proposed Mechanism of Action of GA

context.subject: Medicine
context.topic: Immunology — relapse in autoimmune disease — multiple sclerosis
context.objective: Understand the immunological principles behind disease-modifying treatments in multiple sclerosis, specifically how glatiramer acetate modulates T-cell responses
context.additionalContext: Diagram from: Autoimmune concepts of multiple sclerosis as a basis for selective immunotherapy, PNAS 2004. Shows proposed mechanism of glatiramer acetate (GA) in relapsing-remitting MS treatment.

visualFacts.claim: The diagram is divided into two distinct regions labelled 'periphery' (left) and 'CNS' (right)
The two regions are separated by a vertical element labelled 'blood-brain-barrier'
The diagram shows a progression from a TH1 cell to a TH2 cell in the periphery, indicating a phenotype shift
The TH1 cell has a red interior while TH2 cells have green interiors
A macrophage in the periphery presents GA via MHC to the T-cell's TCR
A microglia cell in the CNS presents CNS antigen (CNS Ag) via MHC to the T-cell's TCR
The TH2 cell in the CNS releases IL-4, IL-10 (antiinflammatory cytokines) and BDNF (neurotrophins)
GA-therapy is represented with yellow lightning bolt symbols
The macrophage presents a different antigen (GA, shown as a blue/cyan shape) from the microglia (CNS Ag, shown as a purple shape)
A boxed label reading 'bystander suppression' is associated with the antiinflammatory cytokine outputs
A boxed label reading 'neuroprotection' is associated with BDNF/neurotrophin output
A dashed arrow connects TH1 to TH2 in the periphery, while a solid arrow shows migration across the blood-brain-barrier
The description should NOT state that TH1 cells are present in or migrate into the CNS; only TH2 cells appear in the CNS region

context.subject: History
context.topic: The Hundred Years War — Siege Warfare and the Conquest of Normandy
context.objective: Understand the military strategies and technologies used in Henry V's conquest of Normandy, including the role of gunpowder artillery in siege warfare
context.additionalContext: Manuscript miniature from Martial d'Auvergne, Vigiles du roi Charles VII (c.1490–1500), depicting the Siege of Rouen (1418–1419).

visualFacts.claim: The image is a medieval manuscript miniature, not a tapestry, photograph, or modern illustration
Multiple cannons on wheeled carriages are depicted as the primary siege weapons, not trebuchets or catapults
The city's fortifications include cylindrical towers with pointed or conical roofs on the right side of the image
Military tents are visible in the background, representing the besieging army's encampment
Armed figures appear on both sides — attackers on the left with siege equipment, and defenders visible near the fortifications on the right
No individuals are identified by name, label, or inscription in the image — the description should not identify specific historical figures by name based solely on visual content
visualFacts.failPattern: henry v is shown|henry v is depicted|henry v appears|figure.{0,20}is henry|identified as henry

visualFacts.claim: The image has a decorative gold or gilded border frame
At least one tower features a Gothic-style window with architectural tracery
A wooden gate or door with visible timber framing appears between the towers
Red sparks or fire effects are visible at cannon muzzles, showing the weapons being discharged
A water-filled moat or ditch runs between the attacking forces and the city fortifications
Some tents in the encampment bear cross markings or symbols

context.subject: Engineering Geology
context.objective: Understand the relationship between return period, service life, and probability of exceedance for different infrastructure types, and how this informs engineering design for geological hazards
context.additionalContext: From: González de Vallejo (2012) 'Design with Geo-Hazards', Soils and Rocks, 35(1). Used alongside definitions of hazard (probability of occurrence of a damaging phenomenon at a specified intensity for a given time within a specific area) and return period (years between two events of similar magnitude).

visualFacts.claim: The image is a nomograph or design chart with smooth continuous curves, not a scatter plot or standard line graph with plotted data points
The vertical axis (Return period, T) uses a logarithmic scale, ranging from 1 to 10,000 years
The horizontal axis (Service life, t) uses a linear scale, ranging from 0 to 250 years
There are six probability curves labelled p = 1%, 5%, 10%, 20%, 30%, and 50%
visualFacts.failPattern: five curves|four curves|three curves|seven curves|eight curves

visualFacts.claim: The p = 1% curve is the highest (requiring the greatest return period for a given service life), while p = 50% is the lowest

Rotated vertical text labels along the lower portion identify infrastructure categories: Buildings, Power stations, Industrial installations, Dams/bridges, and Storage facilities for non-radioactive wastes

Red annotation lines with arrowheads demonstrate how to read the chart: a vertical line at approximately 50 years service life and a horizontal line at approximately 1,000 years return period, intersecting near the p = 1% curve
An embedded formula box shows p = 1−(1−1/T)^t relating probability, return period, and service life
The 'Buildings' category is positioned at approximately 50 years service life
The probability curves are drawn in cyan or blue
Probability values are labelled directly on or adjacent to the curves, with no separate legend box
The chart has a light yellow or cream background within the plot area
